full_transcript.text: So it's for for rocket motors, for like missiles, that's really what we're building for, or artillery shell motors. They use a solid grain. Um it looks actually a little bit like this, uh but it's a solid grain that you can just stick into a a steel case, stick that in the ground, forget about it for 10 years and you can press the button. Uh but what we do differently is is the industry today, the way you make propellant is you have a 30 million dollar mixer, you fill it up with this honey-like substance, it mixes for about 12 hours, then you have 5000 pounds of propellant, you pour that into a mold, put it into an oven, two months later you might have rocket propellant. full_transcript.speaker: Will Edwards

full_transcript.text: Yeah, the App Store of these would be amazing. In their cases, you know, the pedometer, sleep, heart rate, stress monitoring, these things are all kind of very quickly coalesced around a really great product that you would spend $200 a year on. So I think you've got a great GoPro angle and then you've got the kids angle. Kids now are buying Apple Watches, what parents do for safety. They get the LTE Apple Watch so you have GPS and you can do a call with your mom or dad or you can text with them or you can call an Uber. So they kind of, you know, hand set up a watch.

full_transcript.speaker: Jason Calacanis
full_transcript.text: for kids, it's like a big deal. So, really interesting. If you want to go to an incubator, I know a guy running one. I know a couple of guys running them. I'm super fascinated by what you're doing. Come to an accelerator, mine or another, and just do a dev run of a thousand or five hundred of these for a thousand dollars and start getting people excited about what the possibilities are. And there's, there's got to be a thousand developers out there who would want to start playing with this. I think you got a clear path to making an interesting company.

full_transcript.speaker: Will Edwards
full_transcript.text: Thanks, Jason.

full_transcript.speaker: Lon Harris
full_transcript.text: So, I was just going to say, it does feel like to me a real proof of concept of like, we were locked into this idea that visual AI has to be this camera that you're somehow affixing to your front so that it can see everything. And I thought this was such an interesting idea that it could be more subtle, like a thing you're already wearing could just have a little camera in it and now you can see the world.

full_transcript.speaker: Will Edwards
full_transcript.text: Thanks Lon, yeah and Jason, yeah, I'd love to chat with you after the podcast or touch base with you.

full_transcript.speaker: Jason Calacanis
full_transcript.text: They'll give you my phone number right now and we'll talk to you after. No, no, no. I mean, listen, I'm not the king of France over here. I just, people are like, oh, I schedule a call with you, I'm like, or you could just, I give you my phone number and you call me on the phone.

full_transcript.speaker: Lon Harris
full_transcript.text: We'll connect you guys.

full_transcript.speaker: Lon Harris
full_transcript.text: I want to share one more thing. I was checking out Marucci's website and he's got a page. He's like an inventor. I'm going to try to share my screen again. Hopefully it works this time. He's got all of these different kinds of products and gadgets that he's made. There's one I wanted to draw everyone's attention to that if my mom could have hooked me up with an orthodontics wearable that would monitor my daily retainer usage, this would have changed her—

full_transcript.speaker: Jason Calacanis
full_transcript.text: Smart retainer?

full_transcript.speaker: Lon Harris
full_transcript.text: This would have changed her entire life. Every day it was like, Lon, are you wearing your retainer? But I would go to work, go to school all day, she wouldn't know if I was wearing it. A way to electronically monitor my retainer usage would have changed her whole life.

full_transcript.speaker: Jason Calacanis
full_transcript.text: Genius. Absolute genius.

full_transcript.speaker: Alex Wilhelm
full_transcript.text: I think also, has anybody created a Pixel Buds or AirPods case, or the Sonys are really good, I use the Sonys right now, WF-M, whatever they are, 05s. Has anybody created a case that has intelligence in it and an internet connection and Wi-Fi so that you could leave the home with the AirPods and the case and be online and get that experience like the Apple Watch has, and if you leave your phone behind, it's just kind of operating outside of your phone. Has anybody ever built anything like that that you know?

full_transcript.speaker: Will Edwards
full_transcript.text: I don't know of any. Yeah, but these are also things that I've been thinking about in the back of my mind.

full_transcript.speaker: Alex Wilhelm
full_transcript.text: I think this is like could be an incredible asset because when I go skiing, I use a product called Slopes. And when I would use my Apple Watch, I would fire up Slopes on my watch. I didn't need to have my phone with me if I didn't want to, I would, but I could run it on Slopes, it had GPS. And I think that's going to be like a next piece of this, because you need extra batteries. The Slopes really great product and it tracks how many days you ski, what route, your speed on certain routes. It's kind of Strava for whatever, but if that case could be pretty big, it could be like the pack of a cigarette and you could have three—

full_transcript.speaker: Jason Calacanis
full_transcript.text: 60 views. You've got cameras front and back. Have you considered or is it just too bulky, too short battery life to have the cameras facing front and back?

full_transcript.speaker: Will Edwards
full_transcript.text: It could be possible to drop two cameras. I think the challenge there would be then the wireless link between the earbuds and the phone. We barely got two cameras working streaming over Bluetooth, right? And so one of the challenges with bringing this to earbuds is the fact that there's no higher bandwidth protocol like Wi-Fi because that just draws too much power.

full_transcript.speaker: Jason Calacanis
full_transcript.text: Wi-Fi draws too much power, you may not want it in your ears either. So that is a key, which then speaks to intelligence on the AirPods or on the pods themselves. Yeah.

full_transcript.speaker: Jason Calacanis
full_transcript.text: All right, we'll drop you off Maruchi, Kim, and let's keep the show moving. Great job, man. That's a great get. Thank you for getting another guest that I was fascinated by.

full_transcript.speaker: Lon Harris
full_transcript.text: We do what we can.

full_transcript.speaker: Jason Calacanis
full_transcript.text: But you know, just give people a little angel investor advice. Never underestimate anyone is a credo I live by as an angel investor. Why do I live by the credo never underestimate anyone? The people who have the chutzpah and who build things without permission just because they like to tinker or they have some motivation to itch a scratch that they alone have or fix a problem that frustrates them or they see in the world and they think should have been solved already. Well, those people by definition have separated themselves from the rest of humanity. They've moved from the people who are non-playing characters who don't build anything in the world to the group of people who build things in the world. They've moved from passive to active. That is like the great first step. And then there's a step of active to company builders, right? And you know, you could be active and tinkering, but then to want to scale and use the company format in venture capital, that basically is like saying, 'You know, I want to be in the big leagues. I want to go to the NBA or WNBA. I want to be a Navy SEAL. I want to open a restaurant, you know, in New York City. I want to be on Broadway.' You wrote the screenplay, you did the short, but now you want to make the movie, right? And so right before people get very serious about venture capital, they are exactly like Mr. Kim who we just had on. They're tinkering. They're making the orthodontic thing, they're, you know, making these camera buds, they're doing all kinds of interesting projects. And the people who do projects and tinker, today's toys often become tomorrow's innovations and tomorrow's breakout products that change the world. I get the sense that this could be one of those.

full_transcript.speaker: Alex Wilhelm
full_transcript.text: I mean this is what you see I think with so many of these AI tools is at first it's you have to play, you have to tinker, you have to just get interested in it and goof around with it and that's how you figure it out. I don't know if there is a way to figure it out without playing first.

full_transcript.speaker: Alex Wilhelm
full_transcript.text: doing that necessarily at this point.

full_transcript.speaker: Will Edwards
full_transcript.text: Yeah, it's... it's part of the process.

full_transcript.speaker: Lon Harris
full_transcript.text: Yeah, that's how you unlock knowledge with technology I think is it's... somebody can explain it to you all day, but it's really the trial and error is where you unlock things and learn things.

full_transcript.speaker: Jason Calacanis
full_transcript.text: Well, he unlocked that it's really hard to get the images... you know, you can't even listen to a high-fidelity song on AirPods or a phone over Bluetooth, which is why I use... and we'll talk about in off duty, I use certain hardware, certain stack of hardware and high-fidelity hardware in order to experience hi-fi music, which is 20, 30, 40 times as large of a file size, which means it cannot go over MP3. If you're listening to MP3s over Bluetooth, you're listening to 10 to 50% of the quality.

full_transcript.speaker: Lon Harris
full_transcript.text: Right, that's why Neil Young was like, 'I know, I'm founding my own streaming service, Pono,' because this drove him crazy.

full_transcript.speaker: Jason Calacanis
full_transcript.text: And hardware.

full_transcript.speaker: Alex Wilhelm
full_transcript.text: One last word on Ubi, they stream a monochrome 324 by 239 image. That's how they keep it so small. So it does miss some fine details, you could be like, 'what's that thing way over there in the distance,' but for close-up stuff, you know, it's good enough.

full_transcript.speaker: Jason Calacanis
full_transcript.text: Great, we'll see you all on Monday on This Week in Startups Friday. Do me a favor, check out thisweekinai.ai, sign up for the email, follow us, you know we've got to wake up the YouTube channel, so any comments you put under there, you subscribe, you put on alerts, if you thumbs up a video, if you save a video to a playlist to watch later, all of that gives a little signal that YouTube should pay attention to what we're doing, and we'll see you all on Monday, bye-bye.

highlights: Large technology companies are increasingly prioritizing AI infrastructure investments over human headcount, signaling a strategic shift where capital is reallocated from staff to machines to maintain competitive relevance.

The defense industry is entering a critical 24-month window where startups must establish relevance to secure positions in the massive growth cycle driven by innovations like 3D-printed rocket propellant.

What a major corporation views as a "distraction"—typically any market opportunity representing less than 10% of their revenue—represents a massive opportunity for entrepreneurs to build successful standalone companies.

Wearable AI is shifting toward integrating visual intelligence into existing daily habits, such as using camera-equipped earbuds to provide real-time navigation, accessibility for the visually impaired, and proactive safety alerts.

The next frontier in artificial intelligence focuses on Small Language Models (SLMs) and local open-source models that allow businesses to own their data and run specialized processes without relying on massive, centralized hardware.
summary:
This episode of *This Week in Startups* explores the transformative and disruptive power of AI across various sectors, focusing on a macro shift from human-centric workforces to machine-centric efficiency. Host Jason Calacanis and guests discuss the rising importance of Small Language Models (SLMs) and local, open-source AI as the next frontier for startups. A major theme is the strategic pivot by tech giants like Meta and KPMG, which are reallocating capital from headcount toward AI infrastructure. Calacanis views this "machines over people" trend as a crucible moment, encouraging displaced workers to adopt an entrepreneurial mindset by targeting "distraction" problems—market niches under 10% of a giant corporation’s revenue that are ripe for disruption by nimble startups.

The transcript highlights two specific innovations at the intersection of hardware and defense/wearable tech. Will Edwards of Firehawk Aerospace details how his company uses 3D-printed propellant to revolutionize Solid Rocket Motors (SRMs), reducing production times from months to minutes and addressing critical U.S. missile shortages. This segment underscores a broader theme of military transformation, where agile tech startups are beginning to challenge legacy defense "primes." Similarly, Maruchi Kim introduces "Viewbuds"—earbuds with integrated cameras designed to provide subtle, wearable AI assistance. Unlike the "creepy" aesthetic of smart glasses, Viewbuds offer a platform for real-time translation, accessibility for the vision-impaired, and personal safety, illustrating the podcast's overarching message that today's tinkering often evolves into tomorrow’s essential industry innovations.
